name: Lu, Lu
recent_1: Lu, L., Su, B., Jung, S., Qing, L., & Xu, X. (2026). Effects of anthropomorphic features of co-robots on users' perception and emotional responses during human–robot collaboration. Ergonomics, 69(6), 937–951. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2497523
recent_1_synopsis: This study manipulated a collaborative robot's appearance (human-like face, robot-like face, blank screen) and voice (human-like voice, robot-like voice, beeping) to test how anthropomorphic cues shape workers' perceptions and emotional responses during collaboration. It informs the design of co-robot embodiment and communication modalities to improve worker acceptance and psychological comfort in shared workspaces.
recent_2: Su, B., Qing, L., Lu, L., Jung, S., Fang, X., & Xu, X. (2025). Enhancing Data Privacy in Human Factors Studies with Federated Learning. Human Factors, 67(7). DOI: 10.1177/00187208251348025
recent_2_synopsis: This paper applies federated learning to human factors research so that models can be trained across distributed participant datasets without centralizing sensitive physiological or behavioral data. It addresses a growing privacy concern in data-intensive ergonomics and worker-monitoring studies while preserving predictive performance.
recent_3: Jung, S., Su, B., Lu, L., Qing, L., & Xu, X. (2024). Video-Based Lifting Action Recognition Using Rank-Altered Kinematic Feature Pairs. Human Factors, 66(12). DOI: 10.1177/00187208241309748
recent_3_synopsis: This work introduces a computer-vision method that recognizes manual lifting actions from video using rank-altered pairs of kinematic features, enabling markerless ergonomic monitoring of material-handling work. The approach supports automated, low-cost assessment of physically demanding tasks that drive musculoskeletal-disorder risk.
seminal_1: Lu, L., Xie, Z., Wang, H., Li, L., & Xu, X. (2022). Mental stress and safety awareness during human-robot collaboration – Review. Applied Ergonomics, 105, 103832. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2022.103832
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes how robot-related factors elicit mental stress and degrade safety awareness in human workers during collaboration, screening 153 articles and analyzing 25 in depth. With roughly 65 citations (Crossref), it is the author's most-cited work and a foundational reference framing the psychological-safety agenda for collaborative robotics in occupational settings.
seminal_2: Xie, Z., Lu, L., Wang, H., Su, B., Liu, Y., & Xu, X. (2024). Improving Workers' Musculoskeletal Health During Human-Robot Collaboration Through Reinforcement Learning. Human Factors, 66(6), 1707–1722. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231177574
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper develops a model-free reinforcement learning method that adapts robot behavior to improve worker posture and reduce musculoskeletal-disorder risk during collaboration. With about 12 citations (Crossref), it is among the author's most-cited works and demonstrates a learning-based path to ergonomically aware collaborative robots.
expertise_overview: Research centers on human–robot collaboration in industrial and occupational settings, with particular attention to workers' mental stress, psychological safety, and safety awareness when working alongside collaborative robots. A recurring theme is how robot design factors—non-verbal actions, anthropomorphic appearance, voice, and interaction modality—shape human perception, emotional response, and stress, measured through subjective scales (e.g., NASA-TLX) and physiological signals such as galvanic skin response and electromyography. The work also spans occupational ergonomics and musculoskeletal-disorder prevention, including reinforcement-learning controllers that adapt robot behavior to protect worker posture. Methodologically, it draws heavily on machine learning and computer vision, including video-based action recognition of lifting tasks and wearable-sensor analysis for biomechanical risk assessment. More recent directions address data-privacy-preserving methods such as federated learning for human factors datasets. Application domains include manufacturing, manual material handling, and human-centered design of co-robots.
notes: Identified with high confidence. This Lu Lu is a PhD student in the Edward P. Fitts Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering at North Carolina State University (advisor Xu Xu; profile https://ise.ncsu.edu/people/llu4/; prior degrees from Shandong University). Disambiguation from the very common name 'Lu Lu' was anchored on the recurring co-author cluster across all hf_papers and other works—Xu Xu, Ziyang Xie, Bingyi Su, Hanwen Wang, SeHee Jung, Liwei Qing, Yunan Liu—plus the consistent research signature (human-robot collaboration, worker mental stress, musculoskeletal ergonomics, RL, computer-vision action recognition) and the NC State / Edward P. Fitts ISE affiliation. The faculty page lists the exact research focus (human-robot collaboration for safety awareness and mental health via co-robot non-verbal actions), matching the publication record. Every cited DOI was verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching title) and confirmed to list Lu Lu among the authors: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2497523, 10.1177/00187208251348025, 10.1177/00187208241309748, 10.1016/j.apergo.2022.103832, and 10.1177/00187208231177574 (the last four hf_papers DOIs pre-verified). Citation counts are from Crossref is-referenced-by-count as of 2026-06-17 (seminal_1 ~65; seminal_2 ~12); these are typically lower than Google Scholar counts. No unverifiable citations were used. Note: recent_2 (federated learning) and recent_3 (video lifting recognition) are co-authored works where Lu Lu is a middle author; seminal_1 and recent_1 are first-authored. Volume/issue/page for the two Human Factors entries with OnlineFirst-only metadata are given as available; the Ergonomics 2025/2026 article resolves to vol 69(6), pp 937-951.
